chunks.text: John F. Canny is Professor Emeritus at UC Berkeley's EECS department. His contact information includes 637 Soda Hall, phone 510-642-9955, and email canny@berkeley.edu. He has a personal homepage and links to publications, dissertations, and research support. Administrative assistant Leslie Goldstein is located at 765 Soda with phone 3-2469 and email lgolds@berkeley.edu.

John Canny earned his B.Sc. in Computer Science and Theoretical Physics from Adelaide University in 1979, followed by a B.E. (Hons) in Electrical Engineering in 1980. He completed his M.S. and Ph.D. in Electrical Engineering at MIT in 1983 and 1987 respectively. He joined the UC Berkeley EECS faculty in 1987. He authored 'A Variational Approach to Edge Detection' and created the widely used Canny edge detector.

John Canny's research areas include Artificial Intelligence (AI), Control, Intelligent Systems, and Robotics (CIR), Graphics (GR), Human-Computer Interaction (HCI), and Security (SEC). He is affiliated with several research centers: Berkeley Artificial Intelligence Research Lab (BAIR), Berkeley Center for New Media (BCNM), Berkeley Deep Drive (BDD), Berkeley Institute of Design (BID), CITRIS People and Robots (CPAR), and Jacobs Institute for Design Innovation (JIDI).

Selected publications include 'MultiView: Improving trust in group video conferencing through spatial faithfulness' (CHI 2007), 'Camera phone based motion sensing: Interaction techniques, applications and performance study' (UIST 2006), 'Towards context-aware face recognition' (Multimedia 2005), 'Collaborative filtering with privacy' (IEEE Security and Privacy 2002), 'Social tele-embodiment: Understanding presence' (Autonomous Robots 2001), and 'C'mon part, do the local motion!' (ICRA 2001).

Additional publications include 'Part pose statistics: Estimators and experiments' (IEEE Transactions on Robotics and Automation 1999), 'Efficient incremental algorithms for the sparse resultant and the mixed volume' (Journal of Symbolic Computation 1995), 'An opportunistic global path planner' (Algorithmica 1993), 'Multipolynomial resultant algorithms' (Journal of Symbolic Computation 1993), edited book 'Nonholonomic Motion Planning' (1993), 'A fast algorithm for incremental distance calculation' (ICRA 1991), 'Generalized characteristic polynomials' (Journal of Symbolic Computation 1990), 'Solving systems of nonlinear polynomial equations faster' (1989), book 'The Complexity of Robot Motion Planning' (1988), and '2002 AAAI Classic Paper Award winner: A variational approach to edge detection' (1983).

John Canny has received numerous honors and awards: Association for Computing Machinery (ACM) Fellow (2020), Okawa Research Grant (2003), AAAI Classic Paper Award (2002), NSF Presidential Young Investigator (1989), Packard Fellow in Science and Engineering (1988), ACM Doctoral Dissertation Award (1987), and CS Symposium on Foundations of Computer Science (FOCS) Machtey Award (1987).
